result:
The first time I understood that time is not a line but a bruise, I was sitting on a plastic bucket turned upside-down in my grandmother’s toolshed, humidity curling the labels off mason jars. I was supposed to be shelling peas, but the wicker bowl sat between my sneakers like a forgotten planet. Instead, I was listening to the rain on the tin roof, a sound so soft it felt like the sky was apologizing for something it hadn’t done yet. Apologies, like rain, accumulate. That afternoon they pooled inside me until I could feel the past and future pressing against my ribs in exactly the same spot.

My grandfather’s rake leaned in the corner, its tines furred with rust. He had died the previous winter—quietly, as if practicing—leaving behind a collage of unfinished gestures: a half-smoked pipe, a crossword missing the word for happiness in 4 letters, a wristwatch that still exhaled one second at a time. I used to believe objects forgot us faster than we forgot them. Now I think they absorb us the way loam absorbs a spilled cup of coffee: slowly, with dark testimony. The rake was remembering the arc of his arms, the stoop of his back, the small grunt he made when soil resisted. In the shed, the rake and I held vigil for different versions of the same man.

Outside, my mother moved through the garden rows like a bookmark sliding through a family Bible, trying to keep the story straight. She talked to tomato plants because they never interrupted. I could hear her voice through the warped door: “You’re overwatered, sweetheart. You, too, crave more than you can hold.” She meant the plants, but I tucked the sentence under my tongue and carried it for years, a stone to worry whenever thirst arrived. We are all overwatered, I would learn—drenched in memory, in possibility, in newsfeeds that flood the nervous system like sugar dissolving in hot tea. The trick is not to grow roots in the flood itself.

I was eleven, an age when the universe feels transactional: if I collect enough lightning bugs, the summer will never end; if I step on every sidewalk crack, my parents will stay married; if I hold my breath long enough, the gun my best friend’s older brother kept in a shoebox will disappear. None of those bargains held, but the muscle memory of believing still flexes inside me. Sometimes I catch myself bargaining with traffic lights or laptop batteries, whispering deals into the sodium glow of 2 a.m. screens. The child who traded marbles for miracles has merely grown into an adult who trades sleep for answers that never arrive.

Eventually I left the shed, carrying the bowl of unshelled peas like contraband. The clouds had thinned to a bruise-colored silk, and the sun was rehearsing its return. I remember thinking that light is not the opposite of shadow but its negotiation. Every ray is a treaty signed at the edge of an object: you stay, I leave, we meet again at dinner. My mother looked up, cheeks freckled with dirt, and said, “Your hands are empty.” She meant the peas. I almost said, “No, they’re full of rain,” but instead I sat cross-legged in the loam and began popping pods. Each snap sounded like a tiny green bone resetting itself. Inside, the peas were perfect as new moons, and for one unclenched minute I believed we could start over that easily: crack open the day, reveal something smooth and unmarked.

Years later, in a city that smelled of diesel and wet coffee grounds, I would stand on a subway platform listening to a busker play “Somewhere Over the Rainbow” on a steel drum. The notes ricocheted off tiled walls like tropical birds trapped in a mausoleum. Commuters dropped coins without breaking stride, their faces lit by phones broadcasting other people’s curated joy. I thought of the toolshed, of the wristwatch that still ticked though no wrist claimed it, of my mother advising tomatoes. I realized that homesickness is not geographical; it is chronological. We miss not places but moments when the sky still apologized in language we could decipher.

I boarded the train and wedged myself between a woman scribbling equations on a napkin and a man in a hard hat cradling a wilted fern. The fern’s fronds trembled every time the car lurched, as though rehearsing its own death scene. I wanted to tell the man about overwatering, about how we love things into drowning, but I kept quiet. Instead I listened to the equation woman mutter, “Negative b plus or minus the square root of b squared minus 4ac, all over 2a.” Her voice was a rosary of certainty. I whispered the formula along with her, though I hadn’t thought about the quadratic equation since high school. The words felt round in my mouth, like marbles I could trade for one moment of stillness. The train screamed into a tunnel, and for thirty seconds we were all blind. In that darkness, I felt the bruise of time again—tender, alive, mapping every place I had been shoved by accident or choice.

When the lights returned, the man had tucked the fern under his seat as if hiding evidence. The woman had flipped her napkin over, already onto the next problem. I pressed my forehead to the window and watched the tunnel walls smear into gray paint. Somewhere back there, the busker’s rainbow song was still ascending, looking for an exit. I thought: maybe songs are just houseplants we forget to water, maybe equations are prayers with better marketing, maybe my grandfather’s wristwatch is still ticking in a drawer, counting down to the moment I walk back into the shed and find the rake exactly where he left it, ready to finish the motion he began.

The train surfaced. Sunlight spilled across the car like a sudden verdict. Phones emerged from pockets to photograph the shock of day. I closed my eyes and pictured pea pods, rooftop rain, tomatoes arguing against thirst. I understood that we never truly leave a moment; we simply fold it smaller, crease by crease, until it fits in a back pocket. Later, when we sit on overturned buckets or subway seats, we unfold the paper quietly, astonished by how the ink has bled, how the bruise has blossomed into a map that was always pointing here, to this carriage of strangers, to this fern gasping for daylight, to this quadratic lullaby humming under every calculation of arrival.

The doors opened. I stepped onto the platform, into the city’s wet coffee breath, carrying nothing visible. Inside, the bowl of unshelled peas was endless, the rain still apologizing, the rake still describing an arc that never quite ends.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
